name: Pliris Grounded Generation Benchmark
description: Frozen Phase 6 benchmark for comparing final grounded answers across generation-prompt variants. Retrieval contexts must be obtained once per case and reused unchanged across variants.

cases.question: What is requirements traceability, and why is it useful?
expected_behaviors: Define traceability using retrieved evidence.
Explain supported practical value without inventing benefits.
Cite each substantive claim.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent unsupported traceability tools or mandatory templates.
Use uncited factual claims.

cases.question: Which factors should a business analyst consider when prioritizing requirements?
cases.retrieval_query: How should business analysts prioritize requirements?
expected_behaviors: Identify only prioritization factors supported by the context.
Explain the factors concisely.
Avoid presenting one universal ranking formula.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent numeric weights.
Declare a single factor universally decisive.

cases.question: Compare requirements verification and requirements validation.
expected_behaviors: Use a common comparison basis.
Distinguish purpose and focus.
Explain how the practices relate without declaring an unsupported winner.
forbidden_behaviors: Treat verification and validation as interchangeable.
Add unsupported lifecycle sequencing.

cases.question: Compare requirements traceability with requirements maintenance.
expected_behaviors: Compare the practices on evidence-supported dimensions.
Identify similarities and differences.
Avoid treating either practice as a substitute for the other.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent mandatory software.
Declare an unsupported preferred practice.

cases.question: A high-priority requirement changes after approval. How should the business analyst analyze and manage the impact?
cases.retrieval_query: requirements change impact analysis traceability maintenance prioritization
expected_behaviors: Keep scenario consequences conditional.
Identify evidence-supported impact-analysis considerations.
State missing project-specific evidence.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent stakeholders, dates, approvals, or implementation impacts.
Assign unsupported probabilities.

cases.question: A key stakeholder becomes unavailable during requirements discovery. What should the business analyst consider?
cases.retrieval_query: stakeholder analysis business analysis approach risks
expected_behaviors: Distinguish known guidance from scenario assumptions.
Identify evidence-supported considerations and options.
Avoid naming an unauthorized replacement stakeholder.
forbidden_behaviors: Assume governance authority not present in the evidence.
Present a hypothetical delay as certain.

cases.question: Outline a practical requirements traceability matrix.
cases.retrieval_query: requirements traceability relationships lineage impact analysis
expected_behaviors: Provide an outline rather than a completed artifact.
Separate evidence-backed elements from suggested placeholders.
Include traceability and quality checks when supported.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent requirement IDs, owners, dates, or statuses.
Claim a single mandatory template.

cases.question: Outline a stakeholder analysis deliverable for a new initiative.
cases.retrieval_query: stakeholder analysis stakeholder list map personas
expected_behaviors: Describe purpose, audience, sections, inputs, and review needs when supported.
Use placeholders for initiative-specific information.
Keep the response at outline level.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent stakeholder names or influence ratings.
Present the outline as completed analysis.

cases.question: Review the conflict between these sources about the approved requirements baseline.

synthetic_chunks.title: Change Control Standard
synthetic_chunks.text: Approved requirements baselines must be retained. Approved changes are recorded as controlled revisions so prior baselines remain auditable.

synthetic_chunks.title: Delivery Team Procedure
synthetic_chunks.text: When a requirement change is approved, the team replaces the prior baseline file with the newly approved version.

expected_behaviors: Present each source position separately.
Identify a direct contradiction.
Keep the conflict unresolved because authority and supersession are not established.
forbidden_behaviors: Blend the positions into false consensus.
Select a governing source without evidence.

cases.question: Do these sources conflict about who may approve changes?

synthetic_chunks.title: Project Governance Policy
synthetic_chunks.text: The sponsor approves changes that affect committed scope, budget, or delivery dates.

synthetic_chunks.title: Team Working Agreement
synthetic_chunks.text: The product owner may approve backlog wording changes that do not change committed scope, budget, or delivery dates.

expected_behaviors: Present each source position separately.
Recognize the different scopes instead of asserting a direct contradiction.
Avoid inferring hierarchy beyond the supplied text.
forbidden_behaviors: Declare one source superseded.
State that sponsor and product owner authority are identical.

cases.question: Did the pilot reduce operating cost and processing time, and by how much?

synthetic_chunks.title: Pilot Review Note
synthetic_chunks.text: The pilot review observed fewer duplicate entries after the new workflow was introduced. The review did not measure operating cost or processing time.

expected_behaviors: State that cost and processing-time effects are not established.
Mention the supported duplicate-entry observation only if relevant.
Do not convert partial evidence into a complete result.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent percentages, currency values, or time savings.
Claim the pilot reduced cost or processing time.

cases.question: What approval threshold does Pliris use for vendor contracts?
expected_behaviors: Return the exact insufficient-evidence response.
Return no citations.
forbidden_behaviors: Invent an approval threshold.
Use general procurement knowledge.
cases.expected_exact_answer: The available knowledge base does not contain enough evidence to answer this question.
